title: Google Chrome WebGPU Use-After-Free Zero-Day CVE-2026-5281 (CISA KEV): Dawn Component Memory Corruption Enables Sandbox Escape — Fourth Actively Exploited Chrome Zero-Day of 2026, Federal Patch Deadline April 15

timeline.event: CVE-2026-5281 reserved; Google internally discovers active exploitation of Dawn WebGPU use-after-free vulnerability
Google releases emergency patch Chrome 146.0.7680.177/178 for Windows/macOS and 146.0.7680.177 for Linux
CISA adds CVE-2026-5281 to Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog with April 15 federal deadline
Public disclosure: Security researchers confirm active exploitation in the wild targeting Chromium-based browsers
Security advisories published by Qualys, Help Net Security, The Hacker News, and others

simple_summary.what_happened: Google released an emergency security patch for Chrome after discovering a dangerous memory vulnerability being actively exploited by hackers. The flaw exists in Dawn, the part of Chrome that handles WebGPU — the modern API allowing websites to use your computer's graphics card for accelerated tasks. By exploiting a use-after-free bug (where software mistakenly uses memory after it's been freed), attackers can corrupt Chrome's memory and potentially escape the browser's sandbox protection, gaining the ability to run malicious code on the victim's computer. This is the fourth actively exploited Chrome zero-day discovered in 2026, indicating a sustained campaign targeting browser vulnerabilities.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Anyone using Google Chrome versions below 146.0.7680.177 on Windows, macOS, or Linux. All Chromium-based browsers (Microsoft Edge, Brave, Opera, Vivaldi) are also affected. The vulnerability is particularly relevant to organizations that have not enabled automatic browser updates, and federal government agencies face a mandatory patching deadline of April 15, 2026.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attacker sets up a malicious website or injects malicious code into a legitimate website containing specially crafted WebGPU API calls. Step 2: Victim visits the malicious page using Chrome — no clicks or downloads required beyond loading the page. Step 3: The crafted WebGPU code triggers a use-after-free condition in the Dawn component, causing Chrome to access freed GPU buffer memory. Step 4: Memory corruption allows the attacker to manipulate Chrome's internal memory layout, overwriting key data structures. Step 5: Attacker's code escapes Chrome's sandbox and achieves execution of arbitrary commands on the victim's operating system with full user privileges.
what_you_should_do: Update Google Chrome immediately to version 146.0.7680.177 or later — go to Help > About Google Chrome to trigger the update
Update all Chromium-based browsers (Microsoft Edge, Brave, Opera, Vivaldi) to their latest versions as each vendor releases patches
Enable automatic browser updates across all managed endpoints via your endpoint management solution (Intune, SCCM, Jamf)
For organizations: prioritize patching as a P1 incident given active exploitation and CISA KEV listing
Consider temporarily restricting or disabling WebGPU access via enterprise policy if patching cannot be done immediately
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: This is actively being exploited right now — not a theoretical risk. Google confirmed real-world attacks before the patch was released. Chrome is the world's most-used browser (roughly 65% market share), meaning this vulnerability affects billions of devices. CISA's emergency addition to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog and a 14-day federal patching deadline underscore the urgency. The fact that this is the fourth Chrome zero-day exploited in 2026 alone suggests sophisticated, well-resourced threat actors are specifically targeting browser attack surfaces. A successful exploit can give attackers full access to the victim's machine, enabling data theft, credential harvesting, and ransomware deployment.

tactics.description: Attackers deliver the exploit via malicious or compromised websites. Victims are compromised simply by visiting the page in a vulnerable Chrome version — no user interaction beyond page load required.
The use-after-free vulnerability in Chrome's Dawn WebGPU component is triggered via crafted JavaScript/WebGPU API calls, enabling arbitrary code execution within the browser renderer process.
The memory corruption enables sandbox escape, elevating attacker-controlled code from Chrome's sandboxed renderer process to the host operating system level.
The sandbox escape bypasses Chrome's built-in security boundary (renderer sandbox), which is designed to contain malicious code within the browser and prevent host system compromise.

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on identifying unpatched Chrome versions, monitoring for exploit delivery patterns (unusual GPU API usage from web pages), and detecting post-exploitation behaviors following a potential sandbox escape. Browser version monitoring is the primary preventive control.

log_sources.description: Query for Chrome version across all managed endpoints to identify unpatched installations below 146.0.7680.177

log_sources.source: Web Proxy / Secure Web Gateway Logs
log_sources.description: Monitor for connections to known malicious domains delivering browser exploits; look for unusual WebGPU-heavy JavaScript payloads
Monitor for suspicious child processes spawned by chrome.exe or msedge.exe that are not typical browser helper processes, indicating potential sandbox escape
Look for connections to newly registered or suspicious domains that may be hosting exploit delivery infrastructure

what_to_look_for.description: Any Chrome installation below this version is vulnerable to CVE-2026-5281 and should be updated immediately

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unexpected child processes from Chrome/Edge renderer
what_to_look_for.description: Post-exploitation sandbox escape would cause chrome.exe to spawn unusual system processes (cmd.exe, powershell.exe, wscript.exe) not typical for normal browsing

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unusual network connections from browser process
what_to_look_for.description: Post-sandbox-escape, the browser renderer may initiate direct connections to C2 infrastructure bypassing normal browser network stack
what_to_look_for.log_field: process_name='chrome.exe' AND network_destination NOT IN known_google_ip_ranges

what_to_look_for.description: Exploit delivery pages will contain unusually complex WebGPU buffer manipulation code — modern WAFs and browser threat intelligence feeds may flag these patterns

immediate_actions.description: Open Google Chrome and navigate to Help > About Google Chrome. The browser will automatically check for and install the latest update. Restart Chrome after update completes. Verify the installed version reaches 146.0.7680.177 or higher.
immediate_actions.command:
# Check Chrome version on Linux
google-chrome --version
# macOS
/Applications/Google\ Chrome.app/Contents/MacOS/Google\ Chrome --version

immediate_actions.action: Audit and patch all Chromium-based browsers
immediate_actions.description: Microsoft Edge, Brave, Opera, Vivaldi, and other Chromium-derived browsers share the same Dawn WebGPU engine and are also vulnerable. Update each to its latest available version.

immediate_actions.action: Deploy emergency browser patching via endpoint management
immediate_actions.description: Use Intune, SCCM, Jamf, or equivalent to push Chrome 146.0.7680.177+ to all managed endpoints. Given CISA's April 15 federal deadline, treat this as a P1 emergency patch cycle.
immediate_actions.command:
# Intune PowerShell: query unpatched Chrome
Get-InstalledApplication -Name 'Google Chrome' | Where-Object {$_.Version -lt '146.0.7680.177'}

immediate_actions.action: Disable WebGPU via enterprise policy if patching is delayed
immediate_actions.description: If immediate patching is not possible, disable WebGPU using Chrome Enterprise Policy. Set the WebGPU policy to disabled in your Chrome Enterprise policy templates to block the attack surface.
immediate_actions.command:
# Windows GPO registry key to disable WebGPU
reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Google\Chrome" /v WebGPUEnabled /t REG_DWORD /d 0 /f

immediate_actions.description: Query your EDR platform for any instances of Chrome spawning unexpected system processes (cmd.exe, PowerShell, etc.) since April 1, 2026. Treat any such events on unpatched Chrome versions as potential active exploitation.
